The witnesses note Ephraim failing to drive out the Canaanites of Gezer, who dwelt among them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they did not so much as make them tributaries, but probably made a covenant with them, contrary to the will of God. The witnesses note that the Canaanites dwelt in Gezer among them. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the neither did Ephraim — drive out; the the Canaanites in Gezer — left; the dwelt among them — a covenant likely made; the no league with the doomed — make no covenant with them (Exod. 23:32).

The witnesses agree Ephraim, the chief tribe of Joseph, likewise failed — "neither did Ephraim drive out the Canaanites... but the Canaanites dwelt... among them": Gill notes they did not even put them to tribute, but likely made a covenant with them against God's express command (Exod. 23:32); the making of peace with those God had doomed was a direct breach of his word; it teaches that the people of God must make no league with the sin or enemy he commands them to cast out, every such covenant a snare, "thou shalt make no covenant with them, nor with their gods" (Exod. 23:32), the Canaanites dwelling among Ephraim.

So the witnesses make the verse Ephraim failed: The witnesses note the neither did Ephraim: drive out. The witnesses note the the Canaanites in Gezer: left. The witnesses note the dwelt among them: a covenant likely made. The witnesses note the no league with the doomed: make no covenant with them (Exod. 23:32). 30:
"Neither did Zebulun drive out the inhabitants of Kitron, nor the inhabitants of Nahalol; but the Canaanites dwelt among them, and became tributaries." The witnesses note Zebulun failing to drive out the Canaanites of Kitron and Nahalol. The witnesses note that Gill notes Kitron and Nahalol seem to be cities given to the Levites, which perhaps was the reason of their slothfulness. The witnesses note that the Canaanites dwelt among them and became tributaries. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the neither did Zebulun — drive out; the Kitron and Nahalol — levitical cities; the dwelt among them — became tributaries; the the contagion of sloth — evil communications corrupt good manners (1 Cor. 15:33).

The witnesses agree Zebulun joined the sad list — "neither did Zebulun drive out... but the Canaanites dwelt among them, and became tributaries": Gill notes these were Levitical cities, and the tribe's slothfulness in clearing them was the more to be blamed; again the Canaanites were spared for tribute rather than expelled; the repeated failure, tribe after tribe, shows how a common slackness spread through the people; it teaches that the example of disobedience is catching, one tribe's failure emboldening another, and warns against the contagion of a low and compromising spirit, "evil communications corrupt good manners" (1 Cor. 15:33), Zebulun failing to drive out the Canaanites.

31:
"Neither did Asher drive out the inhabitants of Accho, nor the inhabitants of Zidon... nor of Aphik, nor of Rehob:" The witnesses note Asher failing to drive out the Canaanites of Accho, Zidon, and other cities. The witnesses note that Gill notes Accho, the same with Ptolemais, and the other cities Asher did not drive out. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set Asher not driving out the inhabitants of these cities. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the neither did Asher — drive out; the Accho and Zidon — many cities; the Aphik and Rehob — left unconquered; the neglected duty multiplies — unjust in the least, unjust in much (Luke 16:10).

The witnesses agree Asher likewise failed, and at many points — "neither did Asher drive out... Accho... Zidon... Aphik... Rehob": the long list of cities left unconquered shows how widely the tribe fell short of its calling; the more cities spared, the deeper the failure; it teaches that the people of God who do not press the warfare leave the enemy entrenched at every point, the unfought foe holding ground that should have been theirs; it warns that neglected duty multiplies, one omission breeding many, "he that is unjust in the least is unjust also in much" (Luke 16:10), Asher failing to drive out many cities.

So the witnesses make the verse Asher failed: The witnesses note the neither did Asher: drive out. The witnesses note the Accho and Zidon: many cities. 32:
"But the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites, the inhabitants of the land: for they did not drive them out." The witnesses note the Asherites dwelling among the Canaanites, in a worse case than the rest. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were in a worse condition than those before mentioned, for the Canaanites possessed the country and were masters of the cities. The witnesses note that the Asherites dwelt among them, not driving them out. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Asherites dwelt — among the Canaanites; the the inhabitants of the land — the heathen masters; the not driving them out — the worst case; the not conformed — be not conformed to this world (Rom. 12:2).

The witnesses agree Asher's case was the worst yet — "the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites... for they did not drive them out": Gill notes that here the Canaanites were the masters and the Asherites dwelt among them, not they among the Asherites; the tribe sank to living as guests in their own God-given land, the heathen in possession; it teaches the deep degradation of God's people when they make peace with sin — not merely tolerating the enemy but dwelling under his shadow, conformed to the world rather than separate from it; it warns against being mastered by what we should master, "be not conformed to this world" (Rom. 12:2), the Asherites dwelling among the Canaanites.

The verse thus records that the Asherites dwelt among the Canaanites, the heathen mastering the land — the deep degradation of God's people who make peace with sin, dwelling under the enemy's shadow, called not to be conformed to this world (Rom. 12:2).
33:
"Neither did Naphtali drive out the inhabitants of Bethshemesh, nor the inhabitants of Bethanath; but he dwelt among the Canaanites... nevertheless the inhabitants... became tributaries unto them." The witnesses note Naphtali failing to drive out Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath, dwelling among the Canaanites. The witnesses note that Gill refers to the places of Beth-shemesh and Beth-anath. The witnesses note that Clarke and Ellicott note Naphtali not driving them out, the Canaanites becoming tributaries. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the neither did Naphtali — drive out; the Beth-shemesh, Beth-anath — houses of idols; the dwelt among them — the idols' seats left; the the idol a snare — their idols were a snare unto them (Ps. 106:36).

The witnesses agree Naphtali too failed — "neither did Naphtali drive out... but he dwelt among the Canaanites": the names of the cities, "Beth-shemesh" (house of the sun) and "Beth-anath" (house of Anath, a goddess), betray the idolatry that filled them, the very thing Israel was to root out; yet Naphtali dwelt among such places rather than cleansing them; the suffering of these seats of false worship to stand teaches how compromise leaves the idols' houses in the midst of God's people, a standing temptation; it warns that the unremoved idol becomes a snare, "their idols were a snare unto them" (Ps. 106:36), Naphtali dwelling among the Canaanites.

34:
"And the Amorites forced the children of Dan into the mountain: for they would not suffer them to come down to the valley:" The witnesses note the Amorites forcing Dan up into the mountain, not suffering them into the valley. The witnesses note that Gill notes the Amorites forced Dan into the mountainous part of their tribe. The witnesses note that they would not suffer them to come down to the valley. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Amorites forced Dan — into the mountain; the not into the valley — the foe prevailing; the the enemy mastered them — the upper hand; the the unconquered conquers — his servants ye are whom ye obey (Rom. 6:16).

The witnesses agree the failure now turned to open loss — "the Amorites forced the children of Dan into the mountain... would not suffer them to come down to the valley": where the other tribes had let the enemy remain, Dan was driven back by the enemy, the foe gaining the upper hand; the tribe that did not drive out the Canaanite was now itself pressed and confined; it teaches that the sin or enemy God's people fail to subdue will rise and master them, the unconquered foe turning conqueror, "to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are" (Rom. 6:16), the Amorites forcing Dan into the mountain.

35:
"But the Amorites would dwell in mount Heres in Aijalon, and in Shaalbim: yet the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed, so that they became tributaries." The witnesses note the Amorites holding their ground, yet the house of Joseph at last making them tributaries. The witnesses note that Gill notes they would not suffer the Danites into the valley, terrifying them with their iron chariots. The witnesses note that the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed, so that they became tributaries. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Amorites would dwell — stubbornly; the mount Heres and Aijalon — their cities; the the house of Joseph prevailed — by strength; the only to tribute — resist the devil and he flees (James 4:7).

The witnesses agree the Amorites "would dwell" stubbornly in their cities, yet "the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed, so that they became tributaries": at last the stronger tribe of Joseph pressed them down, though only to tribute, not to expulsion; the partial recovery shows that where God's people exert their strength they may regain some ground, yet even here the work was left half done; it teaches that vigour and resolve can roll back the enemy's advance, but that nothing short of full obedience truly clears the land, the foe subdued to tribute still abiding, "resist the devil, and he will flee from you" (James 4:7) — yet the Amorites only made tributary, not driven out.

36:
"And the coast of the Amorites was from the going up to Akrabbim, from the rock, and upward." The witnesses note the bound of the Amorites, from the ascent of Akrabbim, "the scorpions." The witnesses note that Gill refers to the going up to Akrabbim and the rock. The witnesses note that Clarke notes Akrabbim means "of scorpions," likely so called from the number of those creatures there. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the coast of the Amorites — their border; the the going up to Akrabbim — the scorpions; the from the rock upward — still in the foe's hand; the the lurking peril — their poison like a serpent's (Ps. 58:4).

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by marking the Amorites' border "from the going up to Akrabbim" — "the ascent of scorpions," Clarke notes, named for the venomous creatures of that place: it is a fitting close to a chapter of failure, the unexpelled enemy left holding even the scorpion-haunted heights; the naming of so forbidding a bound, still in the foe's hand, seals the sad record of the conquest left incomplete; it teaches that the enemy not driven out remains a sting and a danger in the land, the scorpion-pass a figure of the lurking peril of unconquered sin, "their poison is like the poison of a serpent" (Ps. 58:4) — the Amorite border at the ascent of scorpions, the conquest unfinished.
